message:
chore: Close Phase 1 epic (process_triage-2l3)

All Phase 1 children completed:
- 2kz: Define dormant mode daemon specification
- 6rf: Define golden path UX flow
- 8f6: Define galaxy-brain mode contract
- agz: Define capabilities cache schema
- g7w: Define command and CWD category taxonomies

This unblocks:
- 3ir (Phase 3 - Evidence Collection System)
- iau (Phase 2 - Math Utilities Foundation)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -26,7 +26,7 @@
 {"id":"process_triage-2f3","title":"Create priors.json schema for Bayesian hyperparameters","description":"## Task
Define the JSON schema for priors.json which contains all Bayesian hyperparameters used by the inference engine.

## Background
The inference engine uses conjugate priors throughout:
- Beta distributions for Bernoulli features (orphan, TTY, net activity)
- Beta-Binomial for CPU occupancy
- Gamma distributions for hazard rates and runtime
- Dirichlet for categorical features (command categories, state flags)

Section 4.2-4.5 specifies:
- CPU: p_u,C ~ Beta(alpha_C, beta_C)
- Runtime: t|C ~ Gamma(α_t,C, β_t,C)
- Orphan: p_o,C ~ Beta(a_o,C, b_o,C)
- State flags: pi_C ~ Dirichlet(alpha^state_C)
- Command/CWD: rho_C ~ Dirichlet(alpha^cmd_C)
- TTY: q_C ~ Beta(a_tty,C, b_tty,C)
- Net: r_C ~ Beta(a_net,C, b_net,C)
- Hazard rates: lambda ~ Gamma(α, β) per regime

## Deliverables
- JSON Schema for priors.json
- Default prior values with documentation
- Per-class (useful, useful-but-bad, abandoned, zombie) hyperparameters
- Hazard regime definitions (TTY lost, PPID=1, IO flatline, etc.)
- Version field for schema evolution

## Technical Considerations
- Use rate parameterization for Gamma (shape, rate) not (shape, scale)
- Hyperparameters should be human-editable
- Include comments/documentation fields
- Support machine-level customization (learned priors)

## Example Structure
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "classes": {
    "useful": {
      "prior_prob": 0.7,
      "cpu_beta": {"alpha": 2.0, "beta": 5.0},
      "runtime_gamma": {"shape": 2.0, "rate": 0.01},
      ...
    },
    ...
  },
  "hazard_regimes": [
    {"name": "tty_lost", "gamma": {"shape": 2.0, "rate": 1.0}},
    ...
  ]
}

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

Added priors.default.json with documented default values for all hyperparameters including class priors, hazard regimes (tty_lost, ppid_orphaned, io_flatline, cpu_spinloop), command category Dirichlets, intervention outcome priors.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-2f3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-kze","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T03:43:25.352988044-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-2f3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-2l3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:10:27.951364466-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-2ka","title":"EPIC: Phase 7 - UX Refinement and Premium TUI","description":"## Overview
Implement the premium TUI experience described in section 7 of the plan. This is the user-facing polish that makes pt feel like an 'alien artifact' rather than a script.

## Core Requirements (from Plan Section 7)

### 7.0 Golden Path (One Coherent Run)
- Default `pt` behavior is a single coherent workflow: scan → infer → plan → TUI confirmation → staged execution → after-action summary
- Everything else available as subcommands/flags for experts
- Every run has a durable session_id and artifact directory

### 7.1 Evidence Ledger
- Show top 3 Bayes factors and residual evidence per process
- Posterior, confidence, evidence contributions

### 7.2-7.3 Confidence and Explainability
- Evidence glyphs for I/O, CPU, TTY, orphan, wchan, net
- 'Why' summary: top evidence items and weights
- 'What would change your mind' (VOI hint)

### 7.4 Full-Auto Approval Flow (TUI + Robot Mode)
- Single 'Apply Plan' TUI screen with pre-toggled recommendations
- Drill-down evidence, bulk toggles
- `--robot` bypasses TUI (still honors --shadow and --dry-run)

### 7.5 Premium TUI Layout (Stripe-level in a Terminal)
- Plan-first UI (not list-first): top 'Plan Summary' bar
- Persistent system bar: load/memory/pressure + overhead budget
- Two-pane layout: left = candidates table, right = drilldown (why, ledger, process tree, impact)
- Bottom action bar: Apply Plan, Export Bundle, Render Report, Toggle View, Help
- Progressive disclosure: one-line 'why' by default, full ledger on demand
- Visual language: KEEP/PAUSE/THROTTLE/KILL action badges, SAFE/CAUTION/DANGER risk badges

### 7.6 Interaction Design (Keyboard-First)
- One-keystroke actions: / search, f filter, s sort, enter details, space toggle, a apply, e export, r report, g galaxy-brain, ? help
- Bulk operations with confirmations
- After-action diff showing before/after snapshot and outcomes
- Micro-interactions: staged progress, smooth transitions, inline sparklines

### 7.7 Sharing & Reporting UX
- One command/keybinding to export .ptb bundle and render HTML report
- Reports look like incident dashboards

### 7.8 Galaxy-Brain Mode (Math Transparency + Fun)
- Keybinding 'g' toggles full math view in detail pane
- Shows: posterior by class, posterior odds vs thresholds, expected-loss table, top Bayes factors, per-feature log-likelihood contributions, FDR/alpha-investing state, VOI
- Math cards with equations + substituted numbers + output:
  - Posterior core
  - Time-varying hazard
  - Conformal interval (runtime/CPU)
  - Conformal class set
  - e-values / e-FDR
  - Alpha-investing
  - VOI
- CLI flag: --galaxy-brain or --explain full
- Report tab with same ledger (equations + numbers, respecting redaction)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Default `pt` runs the golden path workflow
- [ ] TUI has two-pane layout with drilldown
- [ ] Galaxy-brain mode toggle works
- [ ] Evidence glyphs and badges are consistent
- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts are implemented
- [ ] After-action diff is displayed
- [ ] Export and report are one-keystroke accessible

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Phase 4 (inference), Phase 5 (decision theory), Phase 6 (action execution)
- Blocks: Phase 16 (documentation)

## Technical Notes
- Use a TUI library like ratatui (Rust) for premium layout
- Galaxy-brain math rendering may use terminal-based LaTeX or ASCII equations
- System bar should update in real-time during scans","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T03:49:51.640093101-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T03:49:51.640093101-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-2ka","depends_on_id":"process_triage-h89","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:05:19.27031212-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-2ka","depends_on_id":"process_triage-sj6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:09:16.988505628-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-2kz","title":"Define dormant mode daemon specification","description":"## Task
Specify the dormant mode daemon (ptd) that provides 24/7 monitoring with minimal overhead.

## Background
Section 3.7 specifies two operating modes:
- Active mode: Full collection + inference + plan (on-demand)
- Dormant mode (ptd): Lightweight monitoring with escalation

Dormant mode mechanics:
- Collect minimal signals at low frequency
- Use sketches/sparse localization for cheap monitoring
- Detect triggers (sustained load, PSI stall, orphan spikes)
- On trigger: quick scan → deep scan suspects → generate session → notify
- Never become the hog (strict overhead budget)

## Deliverables
- Trigger specification (what conditions escalate)
- Overhead budget (max CPU%, max memory)
- Collection cadence and signals
- Escalation protocol
- Cooldown and backoff rules
- Integration specifications:
  - Linux: systemd user service + timer
  - macOS: launchd agent
- Inbox UX for pending plans
- Coordination with manual/agent runs

## Technical Considerations
- Use EWMA + change detection for noise-robust triggers
- Consider time-uniform concentration bounds for sequential testing
- Must acquire pt lock before escalation
- nice/ionice for resource limiting
- Log triggers and escalations for debugging

## Safety Constraints
- Never take destructive actions from daemon
- Default: plan ready for review
- Optional: auto-apply non-destructive mitigations (pause/throttle) if configured

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

-{"id":"process_triage-2l3","title":"EPIC: Phase 1 - Spec and Config Foundation","description":"## Overview
This epic covers all specification and configuration groundwork that must be completed before any implementation begins. This is the architectural foundation that everything else builds upon.

## Background & Context
The current pt tool is a simple bash script with basic heuristics. The transformation plan requires defining comprehensive schemas, contracts, and specifications that will guide all subsequent implementation. Without this foundation, implementation will lack coherence and the alien artifact quality will be compromised.

## Why This Matters
- **Consistency**: All subsequent phases need stable contracts to build against
- **Testability**: Schemas enable automated contract testing
- **Agent Integration**: AI agents need predictable, stable interfaces
- **Auditability**: Explicit specifications enable the galaxy-brain transparency goal
- **Safety**: Policy and guardrail definitions prevent ad-hoc safety implementations

## Scope
1. Package architecture (pt bash wrapper vs pt-core Rust monolith)
2. CLI surface design and stable output formats
3. Golden path UX definition
4. Session model and artifact directory layout
5. All JSON schemas (priors.json, policy.json, telemetry, capabilities)
6. Agent/robot contract definitions
7. Target identity and privilege contracts
8. Bundle/report specifications
9. Dormant daemon specifications
10. Galaxy-brain mode contract

## Success Criteria
- All schemas are defined in versioned JSON Schema files
- CLI surface is documented with stable option names and exit codes
- Contracts are testable (can write contract tests before implementation)
- No ambiguity remains about output formats or behavior modes

## Technical Considerations
- Use JSON Schema for all configuration schemas
- Schema versions must be explicit (semver)
- Exit codes must be automation-friendly (0=clean, 1=candidates exist, etc.)
- Consider backwards compatibility from the start

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

This epic covers all specification and configuration groundwork that must be completed before any implementation begins. This is the architectural foundation that everything else builds upon.

## Background & Context
The current pt tool is a simple bash script with basic heuristics.  {"id":"process_triage-2pil","title":"Implement multi-objective kill set optimization","description":"## Overview
Implement goal-oriented / multi-objective **plan optimization** for selecting a set of actions that achieves user goals with minimal expected risk (Plan §5.14).

This bead is a deeper “optimizer” layer beneath the goal parser and per-candidate contribution estimation.

## Key Objectives
- Minimize total expected loss (primary)
- Achieve resource target(s): memory, CPU, ports, FD count, etc.
- Minimize number of destructive actions
- Respect safety constraints:
  - protected denylist
  - blast radius limits
  - FDR / alpha-investing budgets

## Formulation (Sketch)
Given candidates `i` with:
- expected loss `L_i(a)` for each allowed action `a`
- expected goal contribution `r_i(a)`

Solve (for a single scalar goal R):
```
minimize   Σ_i  L_i(a_i)
subject to Σ_i  r_i(a_i) ≥ R
           a_i ∈ allowed_actions(i)
```

For multiple goals, treat as:
- constrained multi-dimensional knapsack, or
- scalarize via weighted sum with user policy guidance.

## Algorithms (Practical)
- Greedy baseline: sort by (loss / contribution) with guardrails.
- DP exact for small N/targets.
- ILP/LP relaxation (optional) for larger sets.
- Local search to improve greedy solutions.

## UX / Output
- Provide the chosen plan plus 1–3 alternatives showing tradeoffs (Pareto frontier):
  - fewer actions / higher risk
  - more actions / more recovery

This must integrate into the normal plan output:
- `pt --goal ...` (human) and `pt agent plan --goal ...` (agent)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Produces a plan that meets goals when feasible and explains shortfall when infeasible.
- [ ] Respects hard safety constraints and never selects blocked actions.
- [ ] Produces alternative plans/tradeoff view when multiple solutions exist.

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic candidate sets with known optimal solutions.
- Unit: infeasible goals produce correct shortfall reporting.
- Integration: goal-aware plan generation uses optimizer output.

 {"id":"process_triage-2ws","title":"Define bundle and report specifications","description":"## Task
Specify the .ptb bundle format and the premium HTML report generator.

## Background
Section 3.6 specifies shareable artifacts:
- .ptb bundle: Single portable file containing session data
- HTML report: Single static file, CDN-loaded libs, premium visual polish

Bundle format:
- Container: ZIP (cross-platform) or tar.zst (power users)
- Contents: manifest.json, plan.json, telemetry/, raw/ (optional), report.html
- Export profiles: minimal, safe, forensic
- Optional encryption for secure transport

Report requirements:
- Single file that works with file:// (no server needed)
- CDN-loaded libraries with pinned versions and SRI
- Visual polish: dashboard, sortable tables, drilldowns, timelines
- Galaxy-brain tab for math transparency
- Optional DuckDB-WASM for power queries

## Deliverables
- Bundle manifest schema (checksums, versions, redaction policy)
- Directory structure within bundle
- Export profile specifications
- HTML report structure and features
- CDN library stack (Tailwind, Tabulator, ECharts, Mermaid, KaTeX)
- SRI hash requirements
- --embed-assets offline mode specification

## Technical Considerations
- ZIP allows in-browser unpacking via JSZip
- Plan + summaries embedded directly in HTML (not fetched)
- Optional bundle attachment via file picker
- Report should render in under 2 seconds
- Mobile-responsive design

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-2z3g","title":"Implement graph-regularized classification for process dependencies","description":"## Section 4.19 - Graph-Regularized Classification

**Purpose**: Exploit process dependency structure (parent-child, socket connections, shared files) for semi-supervised classification. If parent is zombie, children are likely zombies too.

**Mathematical Background**:
- Graph Laplacian: L = D - A where D is degree matrix, A is adjacency
- Regularized objective: min_f Σ_i loss(f_i, y_i) + λ f^T L f
- Label propagation: f^(t+1) = α D^{-1} A f^(t) + (1-α) Y
- Graph neural message passing: h_v^(k+1) = σ(W h_v^(k) + Σ_{u∈N(v)} h_u^(k))
- Manifold assumption: Decision boundary should not cross high-density regions

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `build_process_graph(processes)` - Edges for parent-child, sockets, files
2. `graph_laplacian(adjacency, normalized)` - Unnormalized or symmetric normalized
3. `label_propagation(graph, labeled_indices, labels)` - Iterative spreading
4. `graph_regularized_loss(predictions, graph, lambda)` - Smoothness penalty

**Why This Matters for pt**:
Zombie detection should respect process trees. If we're 90% sure parent is abandoned, children inherit that suspicion via the graph.

**Integration Points**:
- Process tree analysis (Section 3.3)
- Fleet topology (Section 3.8)
- Dependency-weighted loss (Section 5.1)

**Test Requirements**:
- Verify label propagation converges
- Verify regularization improves accuracy on partially labeled data
- Benchmark on real process trees with known zombies","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead. Canonical implementation: process_triage-nao.9.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:48:00.874822441-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T05:22:43.6716086-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-15T05:22:43.6716086-05:00","close_reason":"duplicate (canonical: process_triage-nao.9)","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-2z3g","depends_on_id":"process_triage-wb3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:56:53.579301593-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-cfia","title":"Implement supervisor detection tests","description":"## Test Requirements: Supervisor Detection (Section 11.7)

### Unit Tests
1. **Parent chain reconstruction**: Test build_parent_chain() correctly traverses PPID links
2. **Supervisor pattern matching**: Test detection of systemd, init, docker-init, containerd-shim
3. **Agent supervisor detection**: Test detection of claude, codex, cursor, aider as supervisors
4. **Shell supervisor detection**: Test detection of bash, zsh, fish as intermediate supervisors
5. **Nested supervisor chains**: Test multi-level supervisor hierarchies (systemd → docker → bash → claude)

### Integration Tests
1. **Process tree reconstruction**: Given real /proc data, correctly build full process tree
2. **Supervisor attribution**: Attribute child processes to correct supervisors
3. **Orphan detection**: Detect when supervisor dies (PPID changes to 1)
4. **Cross-namespace supervisors**: Handle supervisor in different PID namespace

### Edge Cases
1. **Circular PPID references**: Handle malformed /proc with circular references
2. **Missing /proc entries**: Handle processes that exit during scan
3. **Zombie supervisors**: Handle supervisors in Z state
4. **Kernel threads**: Correctly identify and skip kthreadd children

### Test Data Requirements
- Mock /proc filesystem with 50+ process entries
- Supervisor chains of depth 1-10
- Mixed real and containerized processes
- Processes with varying lifetimes (1s to 30d)

### Logging Requirements
- Log each parent chain traversal step
- Log supervisor pattern match results
- Log attribution decisions with confidence scores
- Assert specific log messages in tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-cfon","title":"EPIC: Feature Layer (Deterministic Derived Features + Provenance)","description":"## Overview
Implement the **Feature Layer** from the Alien Artifact plan (Plan §3.2). This layer converts raw collection outputs (ps, /proc, tool events) into **deterministic, provenance-aware derived features** that feed:
- the closed-form Bayesian inference core (posteriors, Bayes factors),
- the decision theory layer (VOI/FDR/alpha-investing, expected loss),
- the explainability ledger + galaxy-brain math cards,
- the telemetry lake (`proc_features`, `proc_inference`) so every run is auditable and reproducible.

This layer is explicitly *not* heuristic scoring. It is a deterministic computation pipeline that turns observations into:
- normalized scalars (e.g., CPU occupancy),
- categorical encodings (cmd/cwd categories),
- time-window summaries (deltas, trends, regimes),
- higher-order analytic summaries (CTW code-length, martingale bounds, marked-point summaries),
with clear provenance metadata so the same features can be recomputed from stored telemetry.

## Background / Why This Exists
The plan’s core promise is **auditable closed-form inference**. That is only credible if we can:
1) reconstruct *exactly what signals were used*,
2) see *exactly how they were derived*, and
3) avoid “hidden” feature logic in random corners of the code.

So: every derived quantity must carry:
- the **input source(s)** (which tool/files),
- the **time window** (Δt, sample count),
- the **identity context** (pid/start_id/uid/boot_id),
- and **parameterization metadata** (e.g., CLK_TCK, N_eff_cores, n_eff adjustments).

## Core Mathematical/Derived Quantities (Plan §3.2)
These are canonical definitions the implementation must adhere to (and show in galaxy-brain views):

- Δt: scan window duration (seconds)
- CLK_TCK: CPU ticks per second (`sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK)` on Linux)
- N_eff_cores: effective CPU core capacity available to a PID (honor affinity/cpuset/quota when available)
- k_ticks: CPU tick delta over Δt (`utime+stime` delta from `/proc/PID/stat` fields 14+15)
- threads: process thread count (`/proc/PID/stat` field 20 or `/proc/PID/status` Threads)
- n_ticks: integer tick budget over Δt:
  - `n_ticks = round(CLK_TCK * Δt * min(N_eff_cores, threads))`
- u: CPU occupancy fraction (clamp to [0,1] for rounding noise):
  - `u = k_ticks / n_ticks`
- u_cores: estimated cores used (can exceed 1):
  - `u_cores = k_ticks / (CLK_TCK * Δt)`

Identity + safety-critical derived fields:
- start_id: stable process start identifier (Linux `/proc/PID/stat` starttime ticks since boot; macOS start time)
- boot_id: host boot identifier (paired with Linux starttime ticks to make start_id meaningful across reboots)

Runtime-model hygiene must be preserved in feature outputs:
- runtime evidence is represented either via a direct Gamma-likelihood feature *or* via a hazard/survival feature set, but the pipeline must avoid generating features that encourage double-counting in the posterior product.

## Requirements
### 1) Feature computation framework
- Deterministic transforms with explicit inputs/outputs.
- Provenance tracking per feature (sources + window + identity tuple).
- Stable schema for feature records; explicit versioning.
- Cross-platform abstraction (Linux/macOS) with graceful degradation when inputs are missing.

### 2) Core derived features (required for early phases)
- CPU delta features (k_ticks, n_ticks, u, u_cores) with N_eff_cores computation.
- Process identity tuple support (pid, start_id, uid, boot_id, plus pgid/sid where needed).
- “unexpected reparenting” indicator `o` that conditions PPID=1 on supervision/session context.
- cmd/cwd category mapping outputs (from taxonomies in `priors.json` / config).
- Basic activity deltas and trend summaries used by inference and decision layers.

### 3) Advanced derived features (integrations)
- CTW prequential log-loss/regret summaries (used for change/anomaly evidence).
- MDL/code-length summaries (Bayes-factor-as-codelength; report ΔL and bits).
- Marked point process summaries (event magnitudes + rates) when event streams are enabled.
- Martingale deviation / confidence-sequence summaries for sustained anomalies.
- Hooks to attach outputs of higher-order extractors (Hawkes, BOCPD, Kalman/IMM, copulas, EVT, sketches) into a single coherent `proc_features` record.

### 4) Telemetry integration
- Emit `proc_features` records that are queryable and reproducible.
- Ensure sensitive raw strings are not introduced here (redaction/hashing is enforced upstream).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] For a fixed captured input window, feature computation is bit-for-bit deterministic.
- [ ] Every feature record includes provenance metadata (inputs + window + identity tuple).
- [ ] CPU occupancy features match the plan definitions, including clamping rules.
- [ ] start_id/boot_id semantics support PID reuse defense and session revalidation.
- [ ] Feature schema is versioned and backward-readable (older runs remain interpretable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for CPU tick math (`n_ticks`, `u`, `u_cores`) including clamp edge cases.
- Unit: N_eff_cores derivation tests (affinity/cpuset/quota present vs absent).
- Unit: provenance metadata tests (sources/window/identity always present).
- Integration: replay captured `/proc` fixture snapshots and verify stable `proc_features` outputs.
- Property-based: invariants like `0 <= u <= 1`, `u_cores >= 0`, `n_ticks >= 0`, monotonicity of window aggregation.
- Logging: tests assert that feature derivation emits structured debug traces when PT_DEBUG/trace enabled (without leaking sensitive strings).

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Feature-layer outputs are stable enough to support inference/decision without ad-hoc patching.
- [ ] Debugging a recommendation never requires re-running: telemetry + provenance can reproduce derived features.
- [ ] Adding a new signal produces a new deterministic feature with provenance and tests (no hidden heuristics).

 {"id":"process_triage-cfon.1","title":"Implement N_eff_cores derivation (affinity/cpuset/quota)","description":"## Overview
Implement the plan’s `N_eff_cores` feature (Plan §3.2): an estimate of the **effective CPU core capacity** available to a process, honoring OS constraints such as CPU affinity, cpuset, and cgroup CPU quota.

This feeds:
- the tick-budget `n_ticks = round(CLK_TCK * Δt * min(N_eff_cores, threads))`,
- the CPU occupancy `u = k_ticks / n_ticks`,
- and downstream Bayesian CPU-occupancy likelihoods.

## Requirements
### 1) Linux sources and precedence
Derive `N_eff_cores` using the best available signals, recording provenance:
- CPU affinity mask (sched_getaffinity or `/proc/<pid>/status` `Cpus_allowed_list`).
- cpuset constraints (cgroup v2 cpuset controller or v1 cpuset cgroup files).
- cgroup CPU quota/period constraints (cgroup v2 `cpu.max` / v1 `cpu.cfs_quota_us` + `cpu.cfs_period_us`).

Combine conservatively:
- `N_eff_cores = min(affinity_cores, cpuset_cores, quota_cores)` when those are known.
- When a signal is missing/unavailable, omit it (do not assume “full machine” unless nothing else is known).

### 2) macOS support (best-effort)
- If detailed per-process affinity/quota signals are unavailable, set `N_eff_cores = logical_cpu_count` but mark provenance as “unconstrained/default”.

### 3) Telemetry + explainability
- Always emit: `N_eff_cores`, `affinity_cores`, `cpuset_cores`, `quota_cores` (nullable), and the derivation path.
- Galaxy-brain must be able to display how `N_eff_cores` was computed for a PID.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `N_eff_cores` matches the plan definition and is conservative under partial information.
- [ ] Provenance is recorded for each contributing constraint.
- [ ] Supports cgroup v2 and common v1 layouts.
- [ ] Fallback behavior is explicit and safe.

## Test Plan
- Unit: parse/compute from fixture files for affinity/cpuset/quota (v2 + v1).
- Integration: run under a constrained cpuset/quota sandbox (if available) and validate derived values.
- Golden: ensure stable outputs (including provenance fields) for fixed fixtures.
- Logging: validate debug output includes the chosen constraint minima.

-{"id":"process_triage-cfon.2","title":"Implement stable process identity features (start_id + boot_id + uid/pgid/sid)","description":"## Overview
Implement the plan’s safety-critical identity features used for TOCTOU/PID-reuse protection and safe action execution.

This bead is **feature-layer** work: compute and persist identity fields that later layers (planning/execution/session resume) use for revalidation.

## Requirements
### 1) Identity tuple fields (Plan §3.2 / §6)
For each observed process, derive and persist:
- `pid`
- `uid`
- `pgid` and `sid` (for group-aware actions)
- `start_id`:
  - Linux: `/proc/<pid>/stat` `starttime` (ticks since boot)
  - macOS: process start time from proc APIs
- `boot_id`:
  - Linux: `/proc/sys/kernel/random/boot_id` (or equivalent)
  - macOS: best-effort boot identifier (or record as null + provenance)

### 2) Normalization and provenance
- Persist the raw source values needed to recompute `start_id` and interpret it (e.g., starttime ticks + boot_id).
- Record whether `start_id` was derived from ticks-since-boot, wall-clock start time, or unavailable.

### 3) Cross-run semantics
- The tuple `(pid, start_id, uid, boot_id)` must be sufficient to detect PID reuse across time and across session resumes.
- When `boot_id` is unavailable, the system must treat revalidation as weaker and tighten safety gates accordingly (policy-driven).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Identity fields are available for every PID we can observe.
- [ ] `start_id` semantics are documented and consistent across the pipeline.
- [ ] `pgid/sid` are persisted and usable for group-aware action planning.
- [ ] Provenance indicates when identity strength is degraded (e.g., missing boot_id).

## Test Plan
- Unit: parse fixtures for `/proc/<pid>/stat` starttime + uid/pgid/sid.
- Integration: spawn short-lived processes and confirm stable identity across sampling windows.
- Safety regression: demonstrate PID reuse defense with a controlled PID-reuse simulation harness (where feasible) or via mocked snapshots.
- Logging: verify identity tuple is emitted in debug logs for action revalidation.

 {"id":"process_triage-cfon.3","title":"Implement command/CWD categorization and normalization outputs","description":"## Overview
Implement deterministic mapping from raw command line + working directory to the **category features** used by priors and explainability:
- `g`: command category (test/dev/agent/shell/build/daemon/unknown)
- `cwd_category`: repo root/temp/unknown/etc.

This is required so Dirichlet-categorical likelihood terms and category-conditioned priors (hierarchical shrinkage) have stable inputs.

## Requirements
### 1) Inputs
- Raw cmdline (redacted upstream; use tokenized/hardened representation here).
- Raw cwd (redacted upstream; use normalized path features here).
- Optional context: repo detection, temp-dir detection, container/workspace context.

### 2) Outputs
- `cmd_category` (`g`) and `cwd_category` as stable enums/strings.
- Stable grouping keys:
  - hashed command signature tokens for grouping in telemetry without leaking secrets.
  - optional short display form (`cmd_short`) for TUI/agent views (must respect redaction policy).

### 3) Rules and pitfalls (Plan hygiene)
- Avoid OS-specific false positives (e.g., macOS `launchd` parenting makes PPID=1 common; cwd patterns differ).
- Prefer explicit pattern lists shipped in config (`priors.json`/`signatures.json`) over ad-hoc heuristics.
- Keep mapping deterministic and versioned.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Category mapping is deterministic and versioned.
- [ ] Categories align with Phase 1 taxonomy definitions.
- [ ] Sensitive strings do not leak into persisted fields (only hashed/grouped forms).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for a curated cmdline/cwd fixture set spanning common developer workflows.
- Integration: ensure mapping results are stable across platforms (where test infra allows).
- Redaction: verify redaction/hashing constraints for cmd/cwd outputs.

 {"id":"process_triage-cfon.4","title":"Implement 'unexpected reparenting' (orphan) feature with supervision/session conditioning","description":"## Overview
Implement the plan’s `o` feature: an **unexpected reparenting / orphan indicator** used as weak evidence for abandonment.

Key plan warning: **PPID=1 is not universally orphan** (macOS launchd, containers, supervisors). This feature must therefore be conditioned on supervision/session context.

## Requirements
### 1) Inputs
- pid/ppid ancestry (from collection)
- supervisor attribution (systemd/launchd/pm2/docker/k8s/tmux/screen/nohup patterns)
- session attribution (controlling TTY, tmux/screen, ssh chain when available)

### 2) Output
Emit a boolean/ternary feature such as:
- `unexpected_reparenting` (true/false)
- plus a reason enum:
  - `reparented_to_init_without_supervision`
  - `pid1_is_supervisor_expected`
  - `container_pid1_expected`
  - `unknown`

### 3) Conservative semantics
- Treat PPID=1 as **weak evidence only** unless other context supports abandonment.
- On macOS, default to `unexpected_reparenting=false` unless there is stronger context.
- Persist the supporting context in telemetry for explainability.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] PPID=1 is not blindly labeled orphan; conditioning logic is implemented.
- [ ] Output is explainable (“why unexpected/expected”).
- [ ] Works across Linux/macOS and in containers.

## Test Plan
- Unit: fixture-based tests covering:
  - systemd-supervised services
  - launchd-managed processes
  - container PID1 cases
  - truly orphaned backgrounded children
- Integration: spawn a child, kill parent, validate classification (where feasible).
- Logging: ensure evidence ledger can show the orphan feature’s rationale.

 {"id":"process_triage-cfon.5","title":"Implement dependency impact score features (sockets/clients/open files/service bindings)","description":"## Overview
Implement the plan’s **dependency impact score** feature used to scale kill-cost in the loss matrix (Plan §3.2, §5.5, §2(Z)).

This is the feature-layer computation of impact inputs; decision theory uses these to inflate expected loss and to gate robot mode.

## Requirements
### 1) Inputs (from evidence collection)
- Live sockets and listen ports (ss/lsof/netstat-derived)
- Client counts / established connections
- Open file descriptors (including write handles)
- Child process relationships / process tree
- Supervisor/service attribution (systemd unit, container id, etc.)

### 2) Output
Emit for each PID:
- `impact_score` (normalized scalar)
- `impact_components`:
  - `listen_ports_count`, `established_conns_count`
  - `open_fds_count`, `open_write_fds_count`
  - `child_count`, `active_child_count`
  - `supervisor_level` (none/systemd/container/etc.)
- `blast_radius_estimates` (optional early version): memory freed, processes affected, ports released.

### 3) Conservative design
- If impact inputs are missing, default to **higher** uncertainty / higher kill-cost inflation, not lower.
- Ensure output is explainable: ledger can list the top impact contributors.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Impact score and components are emitted for all candidates.
- [ ] Missing data increases uncertainty rather than silently lowering impact.
- [ ] Supports group-aware accounting (process tree impact).

## Test Plan
- Unit: fixture parsing for sockets/fds/tree inputs.
- Integration: spawn a listener + clients and verify impact components.
- Safety: integrate with data-loss gate tests to ensure write-fd counts are correct.
- Logging: verify a human-readable explanation string can be generated from components.
","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T05:00:53.577143031-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T05:00:53.577143031-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-cfon.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-cfon","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T05:00:53.578542701-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-cfon.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-cr2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T05:11:50.155435181-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-cfon.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-bxx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T05:11:50.236370472-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-cfon.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-cfon.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T05:11:50.31797668-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-cfon.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-6l1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T05:11:50.397326238-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
